data.body:
PASS 1 review (Cluster 1 plan) — criterion G1G2 (edit-set / scope accuracy). Verified every named target via Read/Grep.

AFFIRMATIONS (G1G2 criteria this chunk passes):
- All named files/symbols EXIST and are correctly cited (no hallucinated targets): `src/rebar/_store/canonical.py` (canonical_str/canonical_bytes at :39-47); `signing.py` `_canonical_payload` (:187-199); `llm/workflow/schema.py` `canonical_json`/`content_hash` (:390-403); `conflict_resolver.py` `_hash_value` (:215-219); `mutation.py` `serialize_manifest` (:89-108). Every "delete the local reimplementation" target is a real `json.dumps(...)`/`hashlib.sha256(...)` site.
- "6 existing event-serialization importers ... unaffected": ENUMERATED + correct. The 6 src importers are event_append.py:33, _commands/delete.py:34, compact.py:28, txn.py:41, inbound_translate.py:218, batch_dispatch.py:211 — all call `canonical_str`/`canonical_bytes` POSITIONALLY with a dict, so adding keyword-only `ascii_only`/`default` params with byte-preserving defaults leaves them untouched. The 3 test importers (test_canonical.py:22, test_e3_txn_bytes.py:19, test_canonical_event_bytes.py:22) likewise call positionally → unaffected.
- Cross-package import direction is SOUND (a plausible scope risk that resolves in the plan's favor): `conflict_resolver.py`/`mutation.py` live under `_engine/rebar_reconciler/` and import siblings via the bare top-level name (`from rebar_reconciler.timeutil import ...`), but the plan's `from rebar._store.canonical import ...` already has live precedent in the SAME package (inbound_translate.py:218, batch_dispatch.py:211). Per docs/architecture.md:100-104 the in-process library never reaches the reconciler as a package import (it's subprocess/`python -m`), but `rebar._store.*` is a real in-process subpackage the engine modules already depend on — so the seam import introduces no new boundary leak.
- Behavioral classification: all four hunks are IN-SCOPE refactors (move existing encoding onto the seam, no new behavior). No CREATION/out-of-scope hunk. AC explicitly forbids a new module and the parent epic freezes output bytes.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] Enumerate the EXISTING characterization tests that lock each migrated symbol's current output, so the migration's "make test passes" is grounded in named pins (not just the new snapshot test). For `serialize_manifest`: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_mutation.py:263-322 (100-permutation stability, sort-key order, `hash == sha256(json_text)`) and tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_differ.py:299-438; note `serialize_manifest` returns a `tuple[str,str]` (json_text, hash) and keeps its local sort/item-shaping — the seam replaces only the inner `json.dumps`+`sha256`, so the AC's byte-equality test must assert on the *tuple's* json_text, not just `content_hash(doc)`. For schema's `content_hash`/`canonical_json`: tests/unit/workflow/test_schema.py:214-218 (order-independence, len==64). For the public re-export surface: llm/workflow/__init__.py:56-57,85-86 lists both in `__all__:69`.
  - criteria: G1G2
  - location: plan AC line 5 (characterization test); plan "What" bullets for mutation.py/schema.py; tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_mutation.py:263-322; tests/unit/workflow/test_schema.py:214-218
  - evidence: `serialize_manifest` (mutation.py:89-108) returns `tuple[str,str]` and shapes/sorts items locally — the seam can only replace its terminal `json.dumps(...,ensure_ascii=True)`+`sha256`; the existing pins assert on `json_text`, so a `content_hash`-only parity check would not cover the manifest text bytes those tests lock.

- [ ] (Cross-ref, already raised under A1 on this epic — restating for the edit-set view) The `conflict_resolver._hash_value` migration as written would change bytes for non-ASCII provenance: current line 216-219 omits `ensure_ascii` → stdlib default `True`, but the seam default is `ascii_only=False` (=`ensure_ascii=False`). The plan migrates it with ONLY `default=str`, so the edit-set is INCOMPLETE — it must also pass `ascii_only=True` (or the AC's snapshot must include a non-ASCII provenance value) for the "byte-identical" claim to hold.
  - criteria: G1G2
  - location: plan "What" bullet for conflict_resolver.py; AC line 3; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/conflict_resolver.py:215-219
  - evidence: current `_hash_value` uses stdlib `ensure_ascii=True` default; seam default is `ensure_ascii=False`; the named migration call (`default=str` only) silently flips ASCII-escaping for any non-ASCII value — a missing param in the edit-set, not a no-op DRY move.
